cancer_type: Asbestos cancer in the lining of the lungs or peritoneum
patient_question: I am being examined because I may have asbestos cancer in the lining of my lungs or peritoneum
canonical_question: How can I get support for the impact of cancer treatment on my sexuality, intimacy, or relationships?
english_summary:
• Asbestos cancer is in the lining of the lungs or peritoneum.
• It is also called mesothelioma.
• Complaints include: being tired, losing weight and difficulty breathing.
• You will undergo examinations, such as a CT scan.
• You will no longer get better from asbestos cancer. With treatments you can live longer or have fewer complaints.
• Asbestos cancer is usually caused by work with asbestos. You can therefore request compensation.
english_full: Asbestos cancer can develop in the lining of the lungs or peritoneum. The pleura is a thin membrane between the ribs and the lungs. The peritoneum surrounds the organs in the abdomen. This disease is rare. About 550 people a year get it. In about 90 out of 100 people who get this type of cancer, it is in the lining of the lungs. In approximately 8 out of 100 people, the cancer is in the peritoneum. You cannot get better from asbestos cancer. You can still receive treatment to live longer or have fewer complaints. Complaints of asbestos cancer in the lining of the lungs: Complaints of asbestos cancer in the peritoneum: inability to defecate properly. Asbestos cancer is usually caused by working with asbestos. In the past, this substance was used in construction, factories and shipyards. About 5 out of 100 people who have worked with asbestos develop this cancer. Very occasionally the disease is caused by a congenital change in the DNA. You usually inherit such a change from one of your parents. We call this a hereditary predisposition. patient_question: I have cervical cancer
canonical_question: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
english_summary:
• Cervical cancer is when cancer cells grow in your cervix.
• Dark red or brown mucus may come from your vagina.
• Blood may also come out of your vagina when you are not having your period.
• You will undergo examinations, such as an MRI and examination of a piece of the cervix.
• Possible treatments include: surgery, radiation, chemotherapy and heat treatment.
english_full: Cervical cancer is when a tumor of cancer cells grows in your cervix. This is called a tumor. The cervix is ​​the narrow part of the uterus. It is located at the bottom of the uterus. Cells from the tumor may appear elsewhere in the body, usually in the lymph nodes in the lower abdomen (pelvis). These are metastases. Sometimes metastases occur in other parts of the body. Such as the lungs, the liver or the bones. The cause of cervical cancer is a virus (HPV). Every year, approximately 900 women in the Netherlands develop cervical cancer. Most women with cervical cancer are between the ages of 35 and 45. Cervical cancer often does not cause any symptoms, especially in the beginning. It is usually discovered during the Pap smear. The symptoms of cervical cancer may include: blood from your vagina when you are not having your period, mucus from your vagina that looks different than normal. The mucus is, for example, brown or black-red. blood from your vagina during or after sex blood from your vagina while you have already gone through menopause You are losing blood even though you have not had your period for a year or more. If cervical cancer is discovered late, the tumor may have grown further into the organs and tissues near the cervix. For example, in the last part of the large intestine (rectum) or the bladder. Then you may experience other complaints, such as: frequent bladder infections blood when urinating blood or mucus in your poop a nagging pain in the lower abdomen

english_full: To make it clear how extensive the cervical cancer is, doctors use a classification with stages. The lower the stage, the greater the chance that you will be cured: The cancer is only in the cervix. The cancer cells are also located in one or more places outside the cervix: 2a: in the upper part of the vagina 2b: in the tissues close to the cervix. There are metastases in the lymph nodes in the pelvis, or along the large body artery. The cancer cells can also be located at the beginning of the vagina (at the opening). The cancer may be in the muscles and ligaments of the pelvic wall. The cancer has grown further in the last part of the large intestine (rectum) or in the bladder. You may be able to heal. Sometimes there are also metastases in the bones, lungs or liver. You can then no longer heal. You can receive treatment. There are 2 treatments for cervical cancer in stage 1 and stage 2a: surgery. The gynecologist removes your uterus, your cervix or part of your cervix. For cervical cancer from stage 2b: chemotherapy with radiation (chemoradiation) radiation with heat treatment (hyperthermia) This is not often done. Each treatment has advantages and disadvantages. You discuss with the gynecologist which treatment best suits your situation. Would you still like to have children? Discuss this with the gynecologist. If you have early-stage cervical cancer, you may be able to choose a treatment that will still allow you to have children. The gynecologist can also refer you to a gynecologist who specializes in fertility. They can discuss the options with you.

You decide together with the gynecologist whether you want treatment and when you want to start it. Chemotherapy is a treatment with medicines. The drugs can kill cancer cells anywhere in your body or prevent them from growing. This means you will have fewer complaints and you may live longer. Side effects of chemo treatment are: nausea and vomiting. You can ask your gynecologist for medication for this. problems with your intestines, such as diarrhea or constipation less good immune system This makes you more likely to become ill due to viruses and bacteria. prickling, tingling, different or less feeling in your hands and feet Chemotherapy can damage the nerves. This can be permanent. tiredness This can last for weeks or months even after the treatment. Radiation therapy is a treatment in which cancer cells are destroyed with radiation. Before the radiation treatment you will receive a CT scan. This is necessary to precisely determine the location of the irradiation. After the scan, you will receive dots on your skin with ink or a tattoo. These dots must remain in place during the period that you are being irradiated. They are necessary to keep you in the right position during the radiation treatment. Then the radiation reaches the right place.

english_summary:

• There are 2 operations where you can still have children:
  ◦ an operation in which the gynecologist removes a piece of the cervix (conization)

english_full: Removal of part of the cervix Removal of the entire cervix With cervical cancer, a tumor of cancer cells grows in your cervix. This is called a tumor. The cervix is ​​the narrow part of the uterus. It is located at the bottom of the uterus. Cells from the tumor may appear elsewhere in the body, usually in the lymph nodes in the lower abdomen (pelvis). These are metastases. Sometimes metastases occur in other parts of the body. Such as the lungs, the liver or the bones. The cause of cervical cancer is a virus (HPV). Every year, approximately 900 women in the Netherlands develop cervical cancer. Most women with cervical cancer are between the ages of 35 and 45. There are 3 operations possible for cervical cancer. Whether you can still have children depends on the operation you have. Which operation you receive depends, for example, on your age, how big the tumor is and whether you still want to have children. The gynecologist will tell you what the risks, benefits and disadvantages of the operations are. Together you decide which operation best suits your situation. You can also ask for advice from a gynecologist who specializes in fertility. They can explain to you what options are available. removing a piece of the cervix (conization). This operation is possible if the cancer is only in the cervix (stage 1). The uterus remains in place. You can still become pregnant after the operation. removing the entire cervix, together with the tissue around it and the upper part of the vagina (trachelectomy). This operation is only possible if you have no metastases in the lymph nodes and the tumor is smaller than 4 centimeters. If the tumor is too large, you may be given chemotherapy before surgery. This treatment can make the tumor smaller so that you can still undergo surgery. The uterus remains in place. You can still become pregnant after the operation. Fertility treatments are often necessary. After this operation, delivery must always be by caesarean section. removal of the uterus After this operation you can no longer become pregnant. You will receive an epidural injection or anesthesia before the operation. With an epidural you will not feel any pain in your lower body. You are awake. With anesthesia you feel no pain and you sleep. The operation is done via the vagina. The gynecologist cuts away a piece of the cervix. Sometimes the gynecologist also removes the lymph nodes in the lower abdomen (pelvis) during the operation. With some tumors the chance of metastases in the lymph nodes is somewhat greater. If this is the case for you, the gynecologist will also remove all the lymph nodes in the pelvis during the operation. After the operation, a doctor (pathologist) examines whether there are cancer cells in the lymph nodes. If so, the gynecologist can advise you on radiation.

patient_question: I have cervical cancer and am having surgery to remove my uterus

english_summary:
• If you have cervical cancer, you can have an operation to remove your uterus.
• Sometimes the doctor removes even more during the operation:
  ◦ the upper part of your vagina
  ◦ the lymph nodes at the bottom of your abdomen
  ◦ the tissue around the uterus and vagina
• Without a uterus you can no longer have children.
• After the operation you may suffer from: problems with urination, changes in sex or psychological problems.
• Discuss these problems with the gynecologist, nurse or your GP.
english_full: Cervical cancer is when a tumor of cancer cells grows in your cervix. This is called a tumor. The cervix is ​​the narrow part of the uterus. It is located at the bottom of the uterus. Cells from the tumor may appear elsewhere in the body, usually in the lymph nodes in the lower abdomen (pelvis). These are metastases. Sometimes metastases occur in other parts of the body. Such as the lungs, the liver or the bones. The cause of cervical cancer is a virus (HPV). Every year, approximately 900 women in the Netherlands develop cervical cancer. Most women with cervical cancer are between the ages of 35 and 45. Women who still want children may be able to have an operation that leaves the uterus in place. english_full: Result of examination: stage With uterine cancer, cancer cells grow in the uterus. There is a layer of cells on the inside of the uterus: the mucous membrane. This is where the cancer usually begins. The cancer can continue to grow. For example, in the muscles of the uterus. Or to other organs, such as the ovaries, vagina, bladder or colon. Cells from the cancer can travel elsewhere in the body, usually in the lymph nodes in the lower abdomen. Very occasionally the cells end up in other organs, such as the lungs or liver. These are metastases. Uterine cancer mainly occurs in women over the age of 50. If you have already gone through menopause: blood or brown mucus from your vagina, even though you have not had your period for a year, blood during urination, weight loss for no apparent reason. If you have not yet gone through menopause: blood from the vagina, if you have not had your period, a lot of blood loss when you have your period, blood during urination, weight loss for no apparent reason. Doctors think that the hormone estrogen is related to uterine cancer. Your body makes this hormone itself. It is important for fertility.

patient_question: I have uterine cancer and still want children
canonical_question: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
english_summary:
• Whether you can still have children depends, among other things, on your age and the extent of the disease.
• There are several ways:
  ◦ the operation only after pregnancy
  ◦ do not have your ovaries removed: you can then try to have a child with a surrogate mother later.
  ◦ have your eggs, fertilized eggs or a piece of ovary frozen
• Are you no longer able to have children? Then you can think about a surrogate mother, adoption or foster care.
english_full: There are several ways to have another child: Sometimes the operation can be postponed. You can then try to get pregnant first. This may be possible if the uterine cancer is discovered very early. You will receive treatment with hormones to treat the disease. You often visit the doctor for check-ups. If the doctor no longer sees the cancer during the checks, you can try to become pregnant. You can receive treatment (IVF) to become pregnant as quickly as possible. You will then go to a gynaecologist. There is a high chance that the cancer will return after pregnancy. That is why the gynecologist recommends that you have your uterus removed after pregnancy. This is only possible if you have no metastases in your ovaries. Then you can discuss with your doctor whether your ovaries can remain in place during the operation. You can start treatment when you want to have a child: The doctor will then remove mature eggs from your fallopian tubes. These can be fertilized in a laboratory with semen from your partner or a donor. The fertilized egg can then be placed in the uterus of a surrogate mother. The doctor then removes mature eggs from your fallopian tubes. These can be fertilized in a laboratory with semen from your partner or a donor. The fertilized egg can then be placed in the uterus of a surrogate mother. This is only possible if the uterine cancer does not need to be treated immediately. You first use hormones for 2 to 3 weeks. You insert the hormones into the skin with a needle. The hormones allow several eggs to mature at the same time. The doctor can see on an ultrasound whether your eggs are ripe. The doctor then aspirates the eggs from the ovaries.

patient_question: I am receiving radiation after surgery for uterine cancer
canonical_question: What should I do immediately after receiving a cancer diagnosis?
english_summary:
• After the operation for uterine cancer you can receive radiation:
  ◦ to destroy cancer cells that remain
  ◦ or to slow down the disease
  ◦ or to reduce your complaints
• You can receive radiation through the skin of your abdomen. Or from a rod or ring in your vagina.
• You will first receive a CT scan. This shows where exactly the radiation should be delivered.
• Radiation therapy can cause side effects, such as problems with urination and a drier vagina.
english_full: Radiation with a rod or ring Radiation through the skin The treatment of uterine cancer almost always starts with surgery. During the operation, the doctor removes your uterus, fallopian tubes and ovaries. After the operation, you may also receive radiation to destroy any remaining cancer cells. This reduces the chance that the cancer will come back. to slow down the disease to reduce your complaints Sometimes the doctor advises to opt for a combination of radiation and chemotherapy. For example, if the cancer grows quickly or after surgery at stage 3. You decide together with your doctor whether you want these treatments. There are 2 types of radiation for uterine cancer. radiation with a radioactive substance through a ring or rod in the vagina (internal radiation) radiation through the skin of the abdomen (external radiation) You usually receive radiation with a ring or rod in the vagina: if you have stage 1 uterine cancer and there is a risk that the uterine cancer will come back You usually receive radiation through the skin of the abdomen: if you have stage 1 uterine cancer that is growing quickly

patient_question: Basal cell cancer
english_full: Basal cell cancer is often on your face, hands or forearms. It almost never spreads to other parts of your body. You can get more spots of skin with basal cell cancer. Use a sunscreen with factor 30 or 50 during the day.

english_full: Basal cell cancer is cancer in the outer layer of your skin. Basal cell cancer is the most common of all types of skin cancer. 1 in 5 Dutch people will ever develop 1 or more spots of basal cell cancer. Especially if you are 30 years or older, the chance of this increases. At first, basal cell cancer looks like a skin-colored or pink bump. The bump can also turn brown or dark red. On the website of skin doctors you will find a number of examples of what basal cell cancer can look like. It is mainly on the skin that has received a lot of sunlight for years, such as: The bump or spot slowly becomes slightly larger. A wound appears in the bump with a scab. The crust comes off easily. It can bleed easily. A bumpy edge may develop around the wound. The bumps may be shiny. The basal cell cancer can also remain flat, often with small flakes. The risk of basal cell cancer is greater in 1 or more of these situations: Your skin is often burned by the sun. You spent a lot of time in the sun as a child. You have had a lot of sunlight on your skin for years. You have had a lot of radiation from a high sun, tanning bed or solarium on your skin.

english_full: Research results Dealing with bladder cancer In the beginning you usually don't notice anything about bladder cancer. Often you only start to experience symptoms if the cancer has been there for a long time. For example, you may notice these things: a burning sensation or pain when urinating losing weight without wanting to do so a palpable lump in your lower abdomen Do you have one or more of these complaints? Then it does not necessarily mean that you have bladder cancer. The complaints can also be caused by something else. In the picture below you can see where your bladder is located in your body. If your doctor thinks you may have bladder cancer, he or she will refer you to the hospital. In the hospital you will see a doctor who knows a lot about the bladder (urologist). There you can have these tests: Examination of your pee (urine). You hand in a jar of pee. Your pee will be looked at under a microscope to see if it contains cancer cells. A CT scan or ultrasound of your bladder and kidneys. Your doctor can see what your bladder and kidneys look like. You may also have a visual examination of your bladder. This is also called a cystoscopy. For this examination, you will first receive an anesthetic gel into your urethra. Then your doctor slides a thin tube or tube with a camera on it through your urethra to your bladder. Your doctor can see on a screen what your urethra and bladder look like from the inside. And whether there is anything visible in your bladder that should not be there. There may still be some blood in your urine in the days after the examination. You may also often feel like you have to urinate. Or have a burning sensation when urinating. This usually goes away on its own. Is there anything visible in your bladder that should not be there? You will then undergo keyhole surgery through your urethra. This keyhole surgery is also called a TUR bladder. Your doctor removes as much as possible of what doesn't belong there. The laboratory will then examine whether it is cancer and what type of bladder cancer it is. Keyhole surgery is therefore an examination and treatment at the same time. Read more about keyhole surgery in your bladder.

Ook kun je vaak het gevoel hebben dat je moet plassen. Of een branderig gevoel hebben bij het plassen. Dit gaat meestal vanzelf weer over. Is er iets in je blaas te zien wat er niet hoort? Dan krijg je een kijkoperatie via je plasbuis. Deze kijkoperatie heet ook wel een TUR-blaas. Je arts haalt zoveel mogelijk weg van wat er niet hoort. In het laboratorium wordt daarna onderzocht of het kanker is en welke soort blaaskanker het is. Een kijkoperatie is dus een onderzoek en behandeling tegelijk. Lees meer over een kijkoperatie in je blaas .

patient_question: I may have bladder cancer and I will have keyhole surgery
canonical_question: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
english_summary:
• Is there anything visible in your bladder that should not be there? You will then undergo keyhole surgery through your urethra.
• Keyhole surgery is an examination and a treatment at the same time.
• During this operation, your doctor removes as much of what does not belong as possible.
• The laboratory will then examine whether it is bladder cancer.
english_full: Results of keyhole surgery Is there anything visible in your bladder that should not be there? You will then undergo keyhole surgery through your urethra. This keyhole surgery is also called a TUR bladder. After this operation you will know for sure whether you have bladder cancer. In the picture below you can see where the bladder is located in your body. You will have an interview with the doctor who arranges the anesthesia (anesthesiologist). Together you will discuss which anesthesia is best for you. There are 2 types of anesthesia: You are put to sleep with medication. This is called anesthesia. This means you won't notice anything during the operation. Or you get a shot in the back. The injection contains an anesthetic so that you no longer feel anything in your lower body. You will be awake during the operation, but you will not feel any pain. The doctor will also tell you how to prepare for the operation. Your doctor inserts a thin tube through the urethra to your bladder. The thin tube has a light and a camera attached to it. Through the tube, your doctor uses a thin metal wire to remove as much of what does not belong as possible. The keyhole surgery usually takes no longer than 1 hour. After the keyhole surgery, the removed part is examined in the laboratory. After the keyhole surgery, you will have a tube in your bladder (bladder catheter). Your pee comes through a tube into a bag. Then you don't have to go to the toilet to pee. The catheter usually remains in place for 1 or 2 days. Your bladder is often rinsed with water or salt water through the tube. This ensures that leftover blood does not stick together. And be thoroughly flushed from your bladder and urethra. You can go home when the catheter is out of your bladder and you can urinate properly again. Sometimes you will be given a bladder irrigation with medication immediately after the keyhole surgery. Rinsing your bladder with medication reduces the chance that the cancer will come back. After keyhole surgery you may suffer from:
original_dutch: Resultaten kijkoperatie Is er iets in je blaas te zien wat er niet hoort? Dan krijg je een kijkoperatie via je plasbuis. Deze kijkoperatie heet ook wel een TUR-blaas. Na deze operatie weet je zeker of je blaaskanker hebt. In het plaatje hieronder zie je waar de blaas in je lichaam zit. Je krijgt een gesprek met de arts die de verdoving regelt (anesthesioloog). Samen bespreken jullie welke verdoving voor jou het beste is. Er zijn 2 soorten verdovingen: Je wordt met medicijnen in slaap gebracht. Dit heet narcose. Je merkt daardoor niets van de operatie. Of je krijgt een prik in je rug. In de prik zit een verdoving waardoor je niets meer voelt in je onderlichaam. Je bent dan wakker tijdens de operatie, maar je voelt geen pijn. De arts vertelt je ook hoe je je kunt voorbereiden op de operatie . Je arts brengt een dunne buis via de plasbuis naar je blaas. Aan de dunne buis zitten een lampje en een camera. Via de buis haalt je arts met een dunne metalen draad zoveel mogelijk weg van wat er niet hoort. De kijkoperatie duurt meestal niet langer dan 1 uur. Na de kijkoperatie wordt het weggehaalde deel in het laboratorium onderzocht. Je hebt na de kijkoperatie een slangetje in je blaas (blaaskatheter). Je plas komt via een slangetje in een zak. Je hoeft dan niet naar de wc om te plassen. De katheter blijft meestal 1 of 2 dagen zitten. Vaak wordt je blaas via het slangetje gespoeld met water of zout water. Dit zorgt ervoor dat restjes bloed niet aan elkaar gaan kleven. En goed uit je blaas en plasbuis worden gespoeld. Je mag naar huis als de katheter uit je blaas is en je weer goed kunt plassen. Soms krijg je meteen na de kijkoperatie een blaasspoeling met medicijnen . Je blaas spoelen met medicijnen maakt de kans kleiner dat de kanker terugkomt. Na de kijkoperatie kun je last hebben van:

patient_question: I have bladder cancer and I am receiving a bladder irrigation with medication
canonical_question: What should I do immediately after receiving a cancer diagnosis?
english_summary:
• During a bladder irrigation you receive fluid containing an anti-cancer drug into your bladder.
• This is done with a tube that goes through your urethra to your bladder.
• You can have 2 types of bladder irrigation:
  ◦ bladder irrigation with chemotherapy
  ◦ bladder irrigation with immunotherapy (BCG)
• After 2 hours you can pee out the liquid.
english_full: Because the cancer can come back, you will usually be treated with bladder irrigation after keyhole surgery. During keyhole surgery, your doctor who knows a lot about the bladder (urologist) removes the cancer. Bladder irrigation with medication reduces the chance that the cancer will come back. You get this if the bladder cancer is only in the inner layer or layers of the bladder. This is the mucous membrane and sometimes also the connective tissue layer. In the picture below you can see the different layers of the bladder. There are 2 types of bladder irrigation. Bladder irrigation with chemotherapy often contains the medicine mitomycin or epirubicin. This destroys cancer cells that may still have been in your bladder after keyhole surgery. And the medicine makes the cancer cells less likely to return. A bladder rinse with immunotherapy contains a medicine that strengthens your immune system. This allows your own immune system to destroy the cancer cells better. Epirubicin is an anti-cancer substance (cytostatic). It inhibits the growth of some tumors. Doctors prescribe epirubicin as chemotherapy for cancer. Mitomycin is an anti-cancer substance (cytostatic). Doctors prescribe mitomycin for certain types of cancer. You can have 1 or more bladder irrigations. You discuss with your doctor how often you will have a bladder irrigation and with which medicine. This depends on several things. Such as: whether the cancer is in one place or more in your bladder, how big the cancer is, how fast the cancer cells grow, how deep the cancer has grown in the layers of your bladder, whether you have CIS bladder cancer. This is a type of bladder cancer in the inner layer of the bladder that grows quickly.
original_dutch: Omdat de kanker kan terugkomen, krijg je na de kijkoperatie meestal een behandeling met blaasspoelingen. Bij de kijkoperatie haalt je arts die veel weet van de blaas (uroloog) de kanker weg. Door de blaasspoeling met medicijnen is de kans kleiner dat de kanker terugkomt. Je krijgt dit als de blaaskanker alleen in de binnenste laag of lagen van de blaas zit. Dit is het slijmvlies en soms ook de bindweefsel-laag. In het plaatje hieronder zie je de verschillende lagen van de blaas. Er zijn 2 soorten blaasspoelingen. In een blaasspoeling met chemotherapie zit vaak het medicijn mitomycine of epirubicine . Hierdoor gaan kankercellen kapot die misschien nog in je blaas zaten na de kijkoperatie. En door het medicijn komen de kankercellen minder snel terug. In een blaasspoeling met immuun-therapie zit een medicijn dat je afweer sterker maakt. Hierdoor kan je eigen afweer de kankercellen beter kapotmaken. Epirubicine is een kankerremmende stof (cytostaticum). Het remt de groei van sommige tumoren. Artsen schrijven epirubicine voor als chemotherapie (chemokuur) bij kanker. Mitomycine is een kankerremmende stof (cytostaticum). Artsen schrijven mitomycine voor bij bepaalde vormen van kanker. Je kunt 1 of meer blaasspoelingen krijgen. Je bespreekt met je arts hoe vaak je een blaasspoeling krijgt en met welk medicijn. Dit hangt af van verschillende dingen. Zoals: of de kanker op 1 plek of op meer plekken in je blaas zit hoe groot de kanker is hoe snel de kankercellen groeien hoe diep de kanker in de lagen van je blaas is gegroeid of je CIS-blaaskanker hebt. Dat is een soort blaaskanker in de binnenste laag van de blaas die snel groeit.

patient_question: I have bladder cancer that grows into the muscle layer. What treatment can I get to get better?
canonical_question: Where can I get reliable advice on nutrition and diet during cancer treatment in the Netherlands?
english_summary:
• These are the two most commonly used treatments:
  ◦ an operation to remove your bladder
  ◦ chemotherapy with radiation
• Very occasionally you can receive radiation from the inside.
• You discuss with your doctor which treatment is best for you.
english_full: Chemotherapy with radiation Radiation from within You can receive treatment to get better if all of these things are true for you: The cancer has not grown outside the bladder. For example, in the abdominal wall or the wall of the pelvis. Or in nearby organs, such as the prostate or vagina. No cancer cells were found in the lymph nodes. No cancer cells were found in other organs. You are fit enough for the treatment. In the pictures below you can see where the bladder is located in your body. And what the different layers of the bladder are. The two most commonly used treatments to get better are: surgery to remove your bladder. This is called a cystectomy. chemotherapy with radiation. This is called chemo-radiation. Very occasionally you can receive radiation from within to get better. Together with your doctor, you can determine which treatment best suits your situation. You discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the treatment. And what you can expect. The bladder cancer decision aid can help with this. You can always choose whether you want treatment. Your own wishes are important. Are you unsure about a treatment? Then discuss this with your doctor and the people you love. One of the treatments is surgery to remove your bladder.
original_dutch: Chemotherapie met bestraling Bestraling van binnenuit Je kunt een behandeling krijgen om beter te worden als al deze dingen bij jou zo zijn: De kanker is niet doorgegroeid buiten de blaas. Bijvoorbeeld in de buikwand of de wand van het bekken. Of in organen in de buurt, zoals de prostaat of de vagina. Er zijn geen kankercellen gevonden in de lymfeklieren. Er zijn geen kankercellen gevonden in andere organen. Je bent fit genoeg voor de behandeling. In de plaatjes hieronder zie je waar de blaas in je lichaam zit. En wat de verschillende lagen van de blaas zijn. De twee meest gebruikte behandelingen om beter te worden zijn: een operatie om je blaas weg te halen. Dit heet een cystectomie. chemotherapie met bestraling. Dit heet chemo-radiatie. Heel soms kun je bestraling van binnenuit krijgen om beter te worden. Met je arts bekijk je welke behandeling het beste past bij jouw situatie. Jullie bespreken de voordelen en nadelen van de behandeling. En wat je kunt verwachten. De keuzehulp blaaskanker kan hierbij helpen. Je kunt altijd zelf kiezen of je een behandeling wilt. Je eigen wensen zijn belangrijk. Twijfel je over een behandeling? Bespreek dit dan met je arts en de mensen van wie je houdt. 1 van de behandelingen is een operatie om je blaas weg te halen.

patient_question: I have bladder cancer and I am having surgery to remove my bladder
canonical_question: Which hospital should I go to for my type of cancer?
english_summary:
• To get better from bladder cancer, it is sometimes necessary to remove your bladder.
• You can sometimes receive chemotherapy before the operation.
• During the operation, lymph nodes and sometimes other organs such as the prostate or uterus are also removed.
• After the operation, your urine usually leaves your body through a urinary stoma or through a new bladder. You discuss with your doctor what suits you best.
english_full: It may sometimes be necessary to remove your bladder. For example, to get better from bladder cancer. Very occasionally your bladder is removed if you can no longer get better. For example, if you are in a lot of pain or are urinating a lot of blood. Then some things happen differently than you read below. In the pictures below you can see where the bladder is located in your body. And what the different layers of the bladder are. During this operation, your doctor removes the bladder where the cancer is located. The lymph nodes near the bladder are also removed. In men, the prostate, seminal vesicles and sometimes the urethra are usually also removed. In women, the uterus, ovaries, part of the vaginal wall and the urethra are sometimes also removed. Your doctor will discuss with you whether this is necessary in your situation. Together you choose what is removed. Sometimes you can receive chemotherapy before the operation. You will then be given medicines that cause the cancer cells to destroy. This way your doctor can better remove the cancer during the operation. The chance is also slightly smaller that the cancer will come back later. Do you and your doctor decide whether or not to undergo chemotherapy before your bladder is removed? This choice card can help you and your doctor make that choice. You can read what you can expect from chemotherapy and what the side effects can be. If your bladder is removed, your pee (urine) must then be removed from your body in a different way. Your doctor usually discusses 2 options with you: a urinary stoma or a new bladder. With a urinary stoma, your doctor uses a piece of small intestine to create a kind of opening on the right side of your abdomen. You can attach a bag to the opening on your stomach. This way you can collect the urine. With a new bladder, your doctor makes a new bladder (neobladder) from a piece of the small intestine. The doctor attaches the new bladder to your own urethra. This way you can continue to urinate through your urethra, just like before the operation. In the pictures below you can see what a urinary stoma and a new bladder look like. Sometimes you can choose between a urinary stoma or a new bladder. Sometimes you can only get a urinary stoma. You discuss with your doctor which method is best in your situation. This decision aid from doctors, nurses and the patient association can help you and your doctor choose between a urinary stoma and a new bladder. A few weeks before the operation, you will have a meeting with the doctor who arranges the anesthesia (anesthesiologist). Read more about what happens before surgery. It helps if you are as fit as possible before the treatment starts. Then you have a smaller chance of problems during and after the treatment. This is what you can do to become fitter:
original_dutch: Het kan soms nodig zijn om je blaas weg te halen. Bijvoorbeeld om beter te worden van blaaskanker. Heel soms wordt je blaas weggehaald als je niet meer beter kunt worden. Bijvoorbeeld als je erg veel pijn hebt of veel bloed plast. Dan gaan sommige dingen anders dan je hieronder leest. In de plaatjes hieronder zie je waar de blaas in je lichaam zit. En wat de verschillende lagen van de blaas zijn. Je arts haalt bij deze operatie de blaas weg waar de kanker in zit. Ook worden de lymfeklieren in de buurt van de blaas weggehaald. Bij mannen worden meestal ook de prostaat, de zaadblaasjes en soms de plasbuis weggehaald. Bij vrouwen worden soms ook de baarmoeder, de eierstokken, een deel van de vaginawand en de plasbuis weggehaald. Je arts bespreekt met je of dit in jouw situatie nodig is. Samen kiezen jullie wat er wordt weggehaald. Soms kun je voor de operatie chemotherapie krijgen. Je krijgt dan medicijnen die ervoor zorgen dat de kankercellen kapotgaan. Zo kan je arts de kanker tijdens de operatie beter weghalen. Ook is de kans dan iets kleiner dat de kanker later terugkomt. Kies je met je arts wel of niet voor chemotherapie voordat je blaas wordt weggehaald? Deze keuzekaart kan jou en je arts helpen om die keuze te maken. Je leest wat je kunt verwachten van chemotherapie en wat de bijwerkingen kunnen zijn. Als je blaas wordt weggehaald, moet je plas (urine) daarna op een andere manier uit je lichaam. Je arts bespreekt meestal 2 manieren met je: een urine-stoma of een nieuwe blaas. Bij een urine-stoma maakt je arts van een stukje dunne darm een soort opening rechts op je buik. Aan de opening kun je op je buik een zakje vastmaken. Zo kun je de urine opvangen. Bij een nieuwe blaas maakt je arts van een stuk dunne darm een nieuwe blaas (neoblaas). De arts maakt de nieuwe blaas vast aan je eigen plasbuis. Zo kun je via je plasbuis blijven plassen, net als voor de operatie. In de plaatjes hieronder zie je hoe een urine-stoma en een nieuwe blaas eruitzien. Soms kun je kiezen tussen een urine-stoma of een nieuwe blaas. Soms kun je alleen een urine-stoma krijgen. Je bespreekt met je arts welke manier in jouw situatie het beste is. Deze keuzehulp van artsen, verpleegkundigen en de patiënten-vereniging kan jou en je arts helpen in de keuze tussen een urine-stoma en een nieuwe blaas. Je krijgt een paar weken voor de operatie een gesprek met de arts die de verdoving regelt (anesthesioloog). Lees meer over wat er voor een operatie gebeurt . Het helpt als je zo fit mogelijk bent voordat de behandeling begint. Dan heb je een kleinere kans op problemen tijdens en na de behandeling. Dit kun je doen om fitter te worden:

patient_question: I got a urinary stoma
canonical_question: How do I get referred to a cancer specialist?
english_summary:
• With a urinary stoma, your urine passes through an opening in your abdomen into a bag.
• A nurse who knows a lot about stomas will teach you how to care for your stoma.
• Many people get used to this after a while and can work, travel and exercise again.
english_full: You can get a urinary stoma after surgery to remove your bladder. With a urinary stoma, your doctor uses a piece of small intestine to create a kind of opening on the right side of your abdomen. The lake runs through that. You can attach a bag to the opening on your stomach. This way you can catch the pee. You empty the bag regularly. With a urinary stoma you can do many things as normal, such as working, traveling, swimming and exercising. Most people get used to it after a while. After the operation, you will receive help to learn to deal with your urinary stoma. A nurse who knows a lot about stomas will teach you how to empty your urinary stoma and keep it clean. And which bags you can use. You will also learn how to care for the skin around your stoma and what to do if something goes wrong. You will also receive tips on how to deal with a urinary stoma in the things you do in your life. A lot changes after the operation. With a urinary stoma you have to get used to a new way of urinating. This can be difficult at first. You may feel insecure, especially when you are somewhere other than home. For example at work or with friends and family. You may be concerned that your urinary stoma is leaking. Or that other people smell something or notice something. That is very normal. The stoma nurse will help you deal with this. You will also learn what you can take with you when you go somewhere, such as extra ostomy bags. Most people can do many things again after a while. Such as working, traveling, exercising and doing things with other people. It sometimes takes some time, but most people find their own way. You can have sex again a while after the operation. You can sometimes feel insecure about your body. For example, about the scar from the operation, the stoma or the smell. That is very normal. It can help to talk about this with your partner, your doctor or the stoma nurse. You're not the only one dealing with this. It can also help to talk to a psychologist who knows a lot about sex (sexologist). Many people gradually find a way to be together and have sex in a pleasant way. You return to your doctor regularly for check-ups. Your doctor will check how your urinary stoma is doing. He also checks how your kidneys are doing. You will also visit the stoma nurse regularly. They will help you with the care of your urinary stoma. Call your stoma nurse if one or more of these things occur: Your stoma changes shape, color or size. The skin around your stoma is red or darker, itches or hurts.
original_dutch: Je kunt een urine-stoma krijgen na een operatie om je blaas weg te halen . Bij een urine-stoma maakt je arts van een stukje dunne darm een soort opening rechts op je buik. Daar loopt de plas doorheen. Aan de opening kun je op je buik een zakje vastmaken. Zo kun je de plas opvangen. Het zakje leeg je regelmatig. Met een urine-stoma kun je veel dingen gewoon doen, zoals werken, reizen, zwemmen en sporten. De meeste mensen wennen er na een tijdje goed aan. Na de operatie krijg je hulp om te leren omgaan met je urine-stoma. Een verpleegkundige die veel weet van stoma's leert je hoe je je urine-stoma leegt en schoonhoudt. En welke zakjes je kunt gebruiken. Je leert ook hoe je de huid rondom je stoma verzorgt en wat je moet doen als er iets misgaat. Ook krijg je tips hoe je met een urine-stoma omgaat bij de dingen die je in je leven doet. Na de operatie verandert er veel. Met een urine-stoma moet je wennen aan een nieuwe manier van plassen. In het begin kan dat lastig zijn. Je kunt je onzeker voelen, vooral als je ergens anders bent dan thuis. Bijvoorbeeld op je werk of bij vrienden en familie. Je bent misschien bang dat je urine-stoma lekt. Of dat andere mensen iets ruiken of iets merken. Dat is heel normaal. De stoma-verpleegkundige helpt je om hiermee om te gaan. Je leert ook wat je kunt meenemen als je ergens heen gaat, zoals extra stoma-zakjes. De meeste mensen kunnen na een tijdje weer veel dingen doen. Zoals werken, reizen, sporten en dingen doen met andere mensen. Het kost soms even tijd, maar de meeste mensen vinden hierin hun eigen manier. Een tijdje na de operatie kun je gewoon weer seks hebben. Je kunt je soms onzeker voelen over je lichaam. Bijvoorbeeld over het litteken van de operatie, de stoma of de geur. Dat is heel normaal. Het kan helpen om hierover te praten met je partner, je arts of de stoma-verpleegkundige. Je bent niet de enige die hiermee te maken heeft. Het kan ook helpen om te praten met een psycholoog die veel weet over seks (seksuoloog). Veel mensen vinden stap voor stap weer een manier om samen te zijn en op een prettige manier seks te hebben. Je komt regelmatig terug bij je arts voor controles. Je arts controleert hoe het gaat met je urine-stoma. Ook kijkt die hoe het gaat met je nieren. Ook kom je regelmatig bij de stoma-verpleegkundige. Die helpt je met de verzorging van je urine-stoma. Bel je stoma-verpleegkundige bij 1 of meer van deze dingen: Je stoma verandert van vorm, kleur of grootte. De huid rondom je stoma is rood of donkerder, jeukt of doet pijn.

patient_question: I got a new bladder
canonical_question: How do I get referred to a cancer specialist?
english_summary:
• If you have a new bladder, your doctor will make a new bladder from a piece of the small intestine.
• A nurse who knows a lot about stomas will teach you how to urinate with a new bladder.
• Many people get used to this after a while and can work, travel and exercise again.
english_full: You can get a new bladder after surgery to remove your bladder. With a new bladder, your doctor makes a new bladder (neobladder) from a piece of the small intestine. The doctor connects the new bladder to your own urethra. This way you can continue to urinate through your urethra, just like before the operation. Because of the new bladder you no longer automatically feel that you have to urinate. That is why you have to go to the toilet at fixed times, even at night. Sometimes it is not possible to urinate properly from your new bladder. Then you have to empty your new bladder yourself with a tube (catheter). Many people get used to it after a while and can do many things again. Such as working, traveling and exercising. After the operation you will still have a tube in your bladder (catheter). The catheter can usually be removed 2 weeks after the operation. A nurse who knows a lot about stomas will then teach you how to urinate with your new bladder. And how you can empty your bladder yourself with a tube (catheter). You will receive explanations, exercises and tips on how to deal with your new bladder. At first you may have difficulty holding your urine. This is especially difficult at night. You can then accidentally lose urine. Most people get used to a new bladder after a while. A lot changes after the operation. With a new bladder you have to get used to a new way of urinating. This can be difficult at first. You may feel insecure, especially when you are somewhere other than home. For example at work or with friends and family. Most people can do many things again after a while. Such as working, traveling, exercising and doing things with other people. It sometimes takes some time, but most people find their own way. You return to your doctor regularly for check-ups. Your doctor will check how your new bladder is doing. He also checks how your kidneys are doing. You will also visit the stoma nurse regularly. This will help you deal with your new bladder. Call your ostomy nurse if you have trouble emptying your new bladder with a catheter. More information about a new bladder: Everything about urology More information about bladder cancer: Bladder or kidney cancer patient association and Kanker.nl Contact with other people with bladder cancer: Bladder or kidney cancer patient association Doctors and copywriters from Thuisarts have created this information with the guideline for doctors about bladder cancer. Read who creates Thuisarts' information. Read what a guideline is and how it is created.
original_dutch: Je kunt een nieuwe blaas krijgen na een operatie om je blaas weg te halen . Bij een nieuwe blaas maakt je arts van een stuk dunne darm een nieuwe blaas (neoblaas). De arts sluit de nieuwe blaas aan op je eigen plasbuis. Zo kun je via je plasbuis blijven plassen, net als voor de operatie. Door de nieuwe blaas voel je niet meer vanzelf dat je moet plassen. Daarom moet je op vaste momenten naar de wc gaan, ook 's nachts. Soms lukt het niet om je nieuwe blaas goed leeg te plassen. Dan moet je je nieuwe blaas zelf legen met een slangetje (katheter). Veel mensen wennen er na een tijdje aan en kunnen veel dingen weer doen. Zoals werken, reizen en sporten. Na de operatie heb je nog een slangetje in je blaas (katheter). Meestal mag de katheter 2 weken na de operatie eruit. Een verpleegkundige die veel weet van stoma's leert je dan hoe je kunt plassen met je nieuwe blaas. En hoe je zelf je blaas kunt legen met een slangetje (katheter). Je krijgt uitleg, oefeningen en tips voor hoe je met je nieuwe blaas omgaat. In het begin kun je moeite hebben om je plas goed op te houden. Dat is vooral moeilijk in de nacht. Je kunt dan per ongeluk urine verliezen. De meeste mensen wennen na een tijdje goed aan een nieuwe blaas. Na de operatie verandert er veel. Met een nieuwe blaas moet je wennen aan een nieuwe manier van plassen. In het begin kan dat lastig zijn. Je kunt je onzeker voelen, vooral als je ergens anders bent dan thuis. Bijvoorbeeld op je werk of bij vrienden en familie. De meeste mensen kunnen na een tijdje weer veel dingen doen. Zoals werken, reizen, sporten en dingen doen met andere mensen. Het kost soms even tijd, maar de meeste mensen vinden hierin hun eigen manier. Je komt regelmatig terug bij je arts voor controles. Je arts controleert hoe het gaat met je nieuwe blaas. Ook kijkt die hoe het gaat met je nieren. Ook kom je regelmatig bij de stoma-verpleegkundige. Die helpt je met het omgaan met je nieuwe blaas. Bel je stoma-verpleegkundige als je problemen hebt met het legen van je nieuwe blaas met een katheter. Meer informatie over een nieuwe blaas: Alles over urologie Meer informatie over blaaskanker: Patiëntenvereniging blaas- of nierkanker en Kanker.nl Contact met andere mensen met blaaskanker: Patiëntenvereniging blaas- of nierkanker Artsen en tekstschrijvers van Thuisarts hebben deze informatie gemaakt met de richtlijn voor artsen over blaaskanker . Lees wie de informatie van Thuisarts maakt . Lees wat een richtlijn is en hoe die wordt gemaakt .

patient_question: I have bladder cancer and I am receiving chemotherapy with radiation
canonical_question: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
english_summary:
• To get better from bladder cancer, you can sometimes receive chemotherapy and radiation.
• This treatment allows you to keep your bladder.
• During the treatment you may experience fatigue, nausea and pain when urinating.
• Your doctor will discuss with you what you can expect and help you if you have complaints.
english_full: Sometimes you can receive a combination of 2 treatments: chemotherapy with radiation. This treatment is called chemo-radiation. With chemotherapy you receive treatment with medicines. These are usually pills to take at home. The drugs can destroy cancer cells. During radiation, as many cancer cells as possible are destroyed with X-rays. By giving this chemotherapy together with radiation, the radiation works better. The cells then become more sensitive to radiation. You can only receive this treatment if you can get better from bladder cancer. Chemotherapy with radiation may be a treatment instead of surgery to remove your bladder. Your doctor will discuss with you whether chemo-radiation is an option for you, so that you can keep your bladder. You will receive consultations and examinations before chemotherapy and radiation. Your doctor who knows a lot about cancer (internist oncologist) will discuss with you which medications you will receive as chemotherapy. And also how often you will receive chemotherapy and what you can expect. Sometimes your doctor will still conduct research. You will have an interview with the doctor who will irradiate you (radiation therapist). The doctor explains the treatment. He will also make a CT scan. This is to see where exactly the cancer is and where the radiation should be delivered. The doctor places small dots of ink on your skin in these places. This way he can find them exactly. After the CT scan, the doctor will know how and how often you should receive radiation. It helps if you are as fit as possible before the treatment starts. Then you have a smaller chance of problems during and after the treatment. This is what you can do to become fitter: Exercise enough. A physiotherapist can help you with this. Eat healthy and make sure you don't lose any more weight. A dietitian can help you with this. It is also important to have your teeth checked by the dentist. Chemotherapy can cause pain and sores in your mouth. It is therefore important that your mouth and teeth are healthy before you start chemotherapy. You will receive radiation to your bladder several times. At certain times you will also receive chemotherapy. There are different schedules when you receive chemotherapy and when you receive radiation. Your doctor will discuss with you what your schedule looks like. You will often be given pills to take at home. Sometimes you receive the medication through a tube into a blood vessel (infusion). For this you go to the outpatient clinic or day care. You will then stay in the hospital for a few hours. Irradiation is a treatment with X-rays. You're under a radiation machine. The rays are focused very precisely on the cancer. However, the radiation can also damage healthy cells near the bladder. You can get side effects from that. You may experience side effects, such as:
original_dutch: Soms kun je een combinatie van 2 behandelingen krijgen: chemotherapie met bestraling. Deze behandeling heet chemo-radiatie. Bij chemotherapie krijg je een behandeling met medicijnen. Dit zijn meestal pillen om thuis te slikken. De medicijnen kunnen kankercellen kapotmaken. Bij bestraling worden zoveel mogelijk kankercellen kapotgemaakt met röntgenstralen. Door deze chemotherapie samen met bestraling te geven, werkt de bestraling beter. De cellen worden dan gevoeliger voor bestraling. Je kunt deze behandeling alleen krijgen als je beter kunt worden van blaaskanker. Chemotherapie met bestraling kan een behandeling zijn in plaats van een operatie om je blaas weg te halen . Je arts bespreekt met je of chemo-radiatie voor jou een optie is, zodat je je blaas kunt houden. Je krijgt voor de chemotherapie en de bestraling gesprekken en onderzoek. Je arts die veel weet over kanker (internist-oncoloog) bespreekt met je welke medicijnen je als chemotherapie krijgt. En ook hoe vaak je chemotherapie krijgt en wat je kunt verwachten. Soms doet je arts nog onderzoek. Je krijgt een gesprek met de arts die je gaat bestralen (radiotherapeut). De arts legt de behandeling uit. Ook maakt die een CT-scan. Dit is om te zien waar de kanker precies zit en waar de bestraling moet komen. Op deze plekken zet de arts kleine puntjes met inkt op je huid. Zo kan die ze precies terugvinden. Na de CT-scan weet de arts hoe en hoe vaak je bestraling moet krijgen. Het helpt als je zo fit mogelijk bent voordat de behandeling begint. Dan heb je een kleinere kans op problemen tijdens en na de behandeling. Dit kun je doen om fitter te worden: Beweeg genoeg . Een fysiotherapeut kan je hierbij helpen. Eet gezond en zorg dat je niet verder afvalt. Een diëtist kan je hierbij helpen. Het is ook belangrijk om je gebit te laten controleren door de tandarts. Door chemotherapie kun je pijn en wondjes in je mond krijgen. Het is daarom belangrijk dat je mond en gebit gezond zijn voordat je aan de chemotherapie begint. Je krijgt een aantal keer een bestraling van je blaas. Op bepaalde momenten krijg je ook chemotherapie. Er zijn verschillende schema's wanneer je chemotherapie krijgt en wanneer bestraling. Je arts bespreekt met je hoe jouw schema eruitziet. Vaak krijg je pillen om thuis te slikken. Soms krijg je de medicijnen via een slangetje in een bloedvat (infuus). Daarvoor ga je naar de polikliniek of de dagbehandeling. Je blijft dan een paar uur in het ziekenhuis. Bestraling is een behandeling met röntgenstralen. Je ligt onder een stralings-apparaat. De stralen worden heel precies op de kanker gericht. Toch kan de straling ook gezonde cellen in de buurt van de blaas beschadigen. Daar kun je bijwerkingen van krijgen. patient_question: I have bladder cancer and I can't get better
canonical_question: Where can I get reliable advice on nutrition and diet during cancer treatment in the Netherlands?
english_summary:
• If you can no longer get better, there are often still treatments available.
• These can result in fewer complaints. And you might live longer.
• Together with your doctor you discuss which treatments you can and want to receive.
• Talk about what you find important in the last period of your life. For example, with your partner, children, friends and doctor.
english_full: It is often difficult to hear that you cannot get better. Your future is uncertain. You don't know how long you can keep doing the things you want to do. Talk about what you find important in the last period of your life. Even if you may still have a few months or years with treatments. For example, talk to your partner, children, friends and doctor. It gives you peace of mind when the people around you and your doctor know what you want. They can then help you with the choices you need to make. They can also give you the care that suits you. Thinking and talking about dying is not easy for most people. It is often difficult for yourself and the people around you to start talking about it. The decision aid Explore your wishes for care and treatment can help you. For example, you may have these complaints: You have pain when urinating or the feeling that you have to urinate often. Urination is not possible or is difficult. You have pain in your stomach, side or pelvis. You have less appetite for food. You can be scared, gloomy or sad. Or you may have trouble sleeping. Has the cancer also spread to other places in your body? Then you may experience other complaints. This depends on where the cancer has gone. And how big the metastases are. In bladder cancer, the metastases are usually in the lymph nodes, lungs, liver or bones. If you can no longer get better, you can sometimes receive treatment to reduce your symptoms. And maybe live longer. For example, you can sometimes receive these treatments: a tube to drain urine from your kidney, medicine for pain, surgery to remove your bladder

Ook kunnen ze je de zorg geven die bij jou past. Denken en praten over doodgaan is voor de meeste mensen niet makkelijk. Voor jezelf en de mensen om je heen is het vaak moeilijk om erover te beginnen. De keuzehulp Verken je wensen voor zorg en behandeling kan je helpen. Je kunt bijvoorbeeld deze klachten hebben: Je hebt pijn bij het plassen of het gevoel dat je vaak moet plassen. Plassen lukt niet of gaat moeilijk. Je hebt pijn in je buik, in je zij of in je bekken. Je hebt minder trek in eten. Je kunt bang, somber of verdrietig zijn. Of je kunt slaapproblemen hebben. Is de kanker ook naar andere plekken in je lichaam gegaan (uitzaaiingen)? Dan kun je nog andere klachten krijgen. Dit hangt af van de plek waar de kanker naartoe is gegaan. En hoe groot de uitzaaiingen zijn. Bij blaaskanker zitten de uitzaaiingen meestal in de lymfeklieren, longen, lever of botten. Als je niet meer beter kunt worden, kun je soms behandelingen krijgen zodat je minder last hebt van je klachten. En misschien langer leeft. Je kunt soms bijvoorbeeld deze behandelingen krijgen: een slangetje om urine uit je nier te laten stromen medicijnen tegen de pijn een operatie om je blaas weg te halen

patient_question: Breast cancer
english_full: Treatments for breast cancer can include surgery, radiation, chemotherapy and anti-hormone or anti-growth protein medications. You usually receive several treatments at the same time or one after the other. You discuss with the doctor which treatment best suits your situation. This depends on the type of breast cancer. And what you consider important. Change of a breast

patient_question: I may have a hereditary predisposition to breast cancer
canonical_question: How do I get referred to a cancer specialist?
english_summary:
• Breast cancer sometimes has a hereditary cause.
• This may be the case if breast cancer, ovarian cancer or fallopian tube cancer runs in your family.
• Your doctor can assess whether you may have a greater risk of breast cancer.
• If the risk is greater, you can have breast X-rays more often.
• Sometimes a geneticist can examine your DNA.
• If you have an error in the BRCA1 or BRCA2 gene, the risk of breast cancer is high.
• You will then receive more checks or you can choose to have your breasts removed.
english_full: Breast cancer can have a hereditary cause. This is the case for 1 in 12 women with breast cancer. The hereditary predisposition is due to an error in a gene, such as: An error in BRCA1 or BRCA2 gives a high risk of breast cancer. And also a high risk of ovarian cancer and fallopian tube cancer. Of 10 women with an error in such a gene, approximately 7 women will develop breast cancer before they are 80 years old. Men with an error in such a gene can also get breast cancer. Men with an error in BRCA2 also have a greater risk of prostate cancer. You have a chance that you have a hereditary predisposition to breast cancer in these situations, among others: if you were diagnosed with breast cancer at a young age (before you were 40 years old) if someone in your family has an error in the BRCA1 or BRCA2 gene (father, mother, child, brother, sister, uncle, aunt, nephew, niece, grandfather, grandmother, half-brother, half-sister) if your mother or sister was diagnosed with breast cancer and was younger than 40 years old if your mother or sister was diagnosed with breast cancer in both breasts and was younger at the time. was then 50 years old if more women in your family have developed breast cancer when they were younger than 50 years old if you have ovarian cancer or fallopian tube cancer if your mother or sister has ovarian cancer or fallopian tube cancer if there is a lot of breast cancer, ovarian cancer, fallopian tube cancer or prostate cancer in your family if a man in your family has breast cancer Men and women can have a gene error that increases the risk of breast cancer. They can pass that gene error on to their children. Does your father or mother have a gene error? Then you have a 1 in 2 (50%) chance that you also have the gene error yourself. Not everyone with a gene error also gets breast cancer.

english_full: Women who have a greater risk of breast cancer can sometimes receive additional breast examination. For example, you can get this if one or more of these things apply to you: You have a hereditary predisposition to breast cancer. And the doctor thinks that the chance that you will get breast cancer at some point in your life is 20 percent or higher. Breast cancer is more common in your family, but no hereditary predisposition has been found. And the doctor thinks that the chance that you will get breast cancer at some point in your life is 20 percent or higher. Doctors call this familial breast cancer. When you were younger than 40, you had radiation treatment to your chest or armpit for something other than breast cancer. You then have a much greater chance of developing breast cancer. From 8 years after radiation you will receive additional breast examination. Men with a greater risk of breast cancer do not receive additional breast examination. Have you had breast cancer before? Or a pre-stage of breast cancer (DCIS)? Then you will have other checks for breast cancer. Do you want to know whether you may have a greater risk of breast cancer? Or are you worried about breast cancer in your family? Then discuss this with your doctor. They can have additional breast examinations done if necessary. Or refer you to a genetics doctor. This can estimate your risk of breast cancer. Together you discuss whether additional breast examination is necessary. Which tests you can receive depends on your age and your risk of breast cancer. Does the doctor think that the chance that you will develop breast cancer at some point in your life is between 20 and 30 percent? Then, when you are 40 to 50 years old, you will receive breast x-rays once a year through your GP (x-ray of your breast: mammogram). If you are 50 years or older, you will have breast photos every 2 years. This can be done through the breast cancer population survey. Does the doctor think that the chance that you will develop breast cancer at some point in your life is between 30 and 50 percent? Then it depends on your age which tests you can have: If you are 35 to 40 years old, you will have an MRI every 1.5 years. And an appointment with the doctor or nurse in the hospital. This depends on the hospital you go to. If you are 60 years or older, you will have breast photos every 2 years. This can be done through the breast cancer population survey. patient_question: I am being examined in the hospital to find out whether I have breast cancer

english_summary:
• You will receive 1 or more tests in the hospital. For example, breast photos or an ultrasound of your breast.
• After the tests, the doctor will know whether you have breast cancer or not.
• Do you continue to have complaints or is there a change in your breasts? Then go to your doctor.
english_full: Do the breast photos from the population survey show anything that could possibly be breast cancer? Then you will be examined in the hospital. You can also have an examination if you have been to your doctor because something has changed in your breast. For example, one or more of these things: You have a lump in your breast. There are flakes on your nipple. Or around your nipple. Your nipple has been pulled in. Brown or bloody fluid comes from your nipple. Or fluid comes out of your nipple after menopause. Also clear or white fluid. There are 1 or more dents in the skin of your chest. Your chest is red and warm and hurts. And you are not breastfeeding or pregnant. You have already been given medication for this against inflammation caused by bacteria (antibiotics). But they didn't help. Your doctor will then refer you to the hospital. In the hospital, you usually receive the examinations at the outpatient clinic for breast examination. This is called the mamma clinic. Do you have a greater risk of breast cancer? You will then receive additional breast examination. The period before the examinations in the hospital can be exciting and annoying. Many people feel restless or sleep poorly. Talk about it with someone you know well. For example, a partner, family member or good friend. At the breast clinic you will receive one or more of these tests: breast photos (x-ray of your breast: mammogram) an ultrasound of your breast an injection to remove cells and fluid from your breast (puncture)

patient_question: I have breast cancer (female)
canonical_question: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
english_summary:
• Choose the treatment that suits you best together with your doctor.
• If there are no metastases, you can usually undergo surgery:
  ◦ an operation in which the surgeon cuts away the breast cancer, followed by radiation
  ◦ or an operation to have your breast removed
• If you have metastatic breast cancer, you can receive treatments to combat the symptoms.
english_full: With breast cancer, a group of cancer cells grows in your breast. You may have complaints, such as a lump in the breast or eczema near the nipple. You may not even notice it and know that you have breast cancer through breast X-rays and other tests. There are different types of breast cancer: The cancer cells are only in one place: in the milk ducts (DCIS) or mammary glands (LCIS). No cancer cells have grown in the fat or other tissue of the breast. Cancer cells cannot travel further into the body (cannot spread). The cancer cells have grown in the fat and other tissues of the breast. This cancer can spread. For example, to the bones or the liver. This type of breast cancer is the most common. Many women get breast cancer: in the Netherlands 12 out of 100 women. Breast cancer also occurs in men. In the Netherlands, approximately 150 men develop breast cancer every year. The treatment is almost the same as for women. Read more about breast cancer (male). New cells are constantly being made in our body. Old cells are cleared away. Sometimes something goes wrong. Cells are created that divide more quickly. This means that new cells are added more quickly. It is not yet clear exactly why this happens in the breast. It is clear that the risk of breast cancer is greater in these situations: As you get older. Breast cancer is most common in women over 60 years of age. If breast cancer runs in your family. You may have a hereditary predisposition to breast cancer. This is the case for 1 in 12 women with breast cancer. There is an error in a gene in your DNA. For example, in the BRCA1 gene or the BRCA2 gene. Such an error increases the risk of breast cancer, and also of ovarian cancer. If you drink alcohol. Alcohol increases the risk of breast cancer. If you smoke. Smoking increases the risk of breast cancer. If you have had the female hormones estrogen and progesterone in your body for a very long time. The risk of breast cancer therefore increases slightly: if you have your period at a young age if you enter menopause very late if you have not been pregnant if your first pregnancy was at a later age if you take the pill for a long time if you continue to take hormones after menopause against menopausal symptoms if you are overweight after menopause
original_dutch: Bij borstkanker groeit er een groep kankercellen in je borst. Je kunt klachten hebben, zoals een knobbeltje in de borst of eczeem bij de tepel. Je kunt er ook niks van merken en door borstfoto's en ander onderzoek weten dat je borstkanker hebt. Er zijn verschillende soorten borstkanker: De kankercellen zitten alleen op 1 plek: in de melkgangen (DCIS) of melkklieren (LCIS). Er zijn geen kankercellen in het vet of ander weefsel van de borst gegroeid. Er kunnen geen kankercellen verder het lichaam ingaan (niet uitzaaien). De kankercellen zijn in het vet en ander weefsel van de borst gegroeid. Deze kanker kan uitzaaien. Bijvoorbeeld naar de botten of de lever. Deze soort borstkanker komt het meeste voor. Veel vrouwen krijgen borstkanker: in Nederland 12 van de 100 vrouwen. Borstkanker komt ook voor bij mannen. In Nederland krijgen elk jaar ongeveer 150 mannen borstkanker. De behandeling is bijna hetzelfde als bij vrouwen. Lees verder bij borstkanker (man) . In ons lichaam worden steeds nieuwe cellen gemaakt. Oude cellen worden opgeruimd. Soms gaat daarbij iets mis. Er ontstaan cellen die sneller delen. Daardoor komen er sneller nieuwe cellen bij. Waardoor dit in de borst gebeurt, is nog niet precies duidelijk. Wel is duidelijk dat de kans op borstkanker groter is in deze situaties: Als je ouder wordt. Borstkanker komt het meeste voor bij vrouwen ouder dan 60 jaar. Als borstkanker in je familie voorkomt. Je kunt een erfelijke aanleg hebben voor borstkanker . Dit is zo bij 1 van de 12 vrouwen met borstkanker. In je DNA zit dan een fout in een gen. Bijvoorbeeld in het gen BRCA1 of het gen BRCA2. patient_question: I have breast cancer (male)
canonical_question: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
english_summary:
• In the Netherlands, approximately 150 men develop breast cancer every year.
• The treatment of breast cancer in men is almost the same as in women.
• You will usually have an operation to remove the entire breast containing the cancer.
• You will receive other treatments before or after.
• You discuss with the doctor which treatment suits you best.
english_full: With breast cancer, cancer grows in your breast. In men, under one of both nipples, or under both. Or in the skin of the breast. In the Netherlands, approximately 150 men develop breast cancer every year. The treatment is almost the same as for women. Most men who get breast cancer are over 60 years old. Often more people in the family have breast cancer. The breast cancer is then hereditary. If you have complaints consistent with breast cancer, your GP will refer you to the hospital. There you can have these tests: an X-ray of your breast (mammogram) an ultrasound of the breast You can also have these tests: a puncture: the doctor sucks cells and fluid from your breast with a needle. And have it tested in the laboratory to see if it contains cancer. a biopsy: the doctor removes a very small piece from your breast with a needle. And have it tested in the laboratory to see if it contains cancer. After the tests, you will hear how serious your breast cancer is. Doctors use a number for this (the stage). Stage 1 is the least serious, stage 4 the worst. The research also shows which type of breast cancer you have. There are these types: Cancer that originated in the milk ducts. Most men have this type. Cancer that has originated in the mammary glands. Cells that can become cancer, but are not yet. The medical name for this is DCIS. The treatments for men with breast cancer are almost the same as for women.

And a piece of healthy breast around it. The doctor tries to leave as much of your breast as possible. That is why the operation is called breast-conserving. The doctor can usually restore the shape of your breast during the same operation. You will still see a scar later. The operation takes less than 2 hours. You can usually go home the next day. It will then take a few weeks for the wound and the area around the operation to heal again. After this operation you will receive radiation. Irradiation takes a few minutes. You usually go to the hospital 5 times a week for radiation treatment. This should be done for 3 to 5 weeks in a row. Many women with breast cancer can have breast-conserving surgery. The doctor removes the entire breast, including the nipple and areola. During or after the operation you can usually have a fake breast placed in your body. The operation takes a few hours. You can usually go home after about 4 days. It will then take a few weeks for the wound and the area around your breast to heal again. Even after that, you won't be able to do everything for a while. You do not always receive radiation after a mastectomy. But sometimes it does. Will you receive radiation? Then it may be better to wait a few months before having a fake breast inserted into your body. A mastectomy may be the best choice. For example, if your breast is small and the cancer is large. For example in men. You discuss with the doctor which operation you can have. These things are important: How big is the cancer in your breast? The doctor will remove the entire breast if the cancer is very large. Or if the cancer has unclear edges. Is the cancer in one place? Or are there more areas of cancer in your breast? If there are more areas of cancer, it is better to remove the entire breast. Can you get radiation? For example, radiation may not be safe for your baby if you are pregnant. Radiation is always necessary after breast-conserving surgery. What is the most beautiful thing about you? Discuss with the doctor what is probably best for you. Do you have a hereditary form of breast cancer? Some women then consciously choose to have the entire breast removed. Which operation you get also depends on what you consider important. For example, what your breasts look like after the operation. Or how quickly you get better after the operation. And whether you still need radiation or medication.

english_full: Advice before the operation Restore breast shape Complaints after the operation Advice after the operation Most women with breast cancer can have an operation in which they keep part of their breast. This is called breast-conserving surgery. This is almost never possible in men with breast cancer. For example, whether you can have breast-conserving surgery depends on these things: Whether the cancer is small, compared to your breast. Because then most of the breast can remain after the doctor has removed the cancer. Or the doctor thinks it can make your breast beautiful again. Whether you can receive radiation. Because after breast-conserving surgery, radiation is usually necessary. Whether your breast has previously been irradiated or operated on. You discuss with the doctor whether you want and can have breast-conserving surgery. Sometimes you will receive treatment before the operation to reduce the size of the cancer in your breast. For example chemotherapy. This means the doctor doesn't have to cut out as much of your breast. Your doctor will discuss with you what is best in your situation. If the cancer cannot be felt clearly, you will receive something in your breast so that the doctor knows where the cancer is during the operation. For example, a thin iron wire, or a small magnet, or liquid that emits radiation. You will receive this in the hospital on the day of the operation, or 1 or more days before. You will be examined to determine which lymph node the doctor should remove during the operation. You will receive an injection in your chest with a little liquid that emits radiation. You will then receive a scan that shows the lymph nodes. The doctor then knows to which lymph node the cancer can spread first. And it can be removed during the operation. Doctors call this lymph node the sentinel lymph node. The sentinel node is usually located in your armpit. Sometimes in a different place near your chest, such as next to your breastbone or collarbone.
